topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence of the apartment was a physical weight pressing down on Clara. She stared at the bouquet resting on her kitchen counter—a vibrant, almost offensively cheerful arrangement of sunflowers and deep crimson roses. There was no note, just the lush, undeniable presence of the blooms. Her fingers, trembling slightly, traced the velvety texture of a petal, a strange, sharp ache blooming behind her ribs. She hadn't seen Liam in three weeks, and the sight of the flowers felt like a deliberate, cruel punctuation mark on the end of everything. She picked up a stray leaf, crushing it under her thumb, the small act of destruction a futile attempt to disrupt the overwhelming sense of emptiness.

[story 2]

Mark watched the delivery driver leave the package, his gaze fixed on the porch where the vibrant arrangement sat beside the welcome mat. His chest felt tight, a constricted space where his usual steady rhythm had gone erratic. He didn't move toward it. Instead, he leaned against the doorframe, his knuckles white where he gripped the wood. The colors—so bright, so aggressively alive—only amplified the dull, pervasive ache in his core. He could feel a sudden, hot sting behind his eyes, and he blinked rapidly, trying to scrub the sudden moisture away, but the feeling persisted, heavy and suffocating.

[story 3]

"They're beautiful," whispered Sarah, her voice thin and brittle, as she held the vase. The arrangement was opulent, a cascade of perfect, expensive blooms. She didn't look at the recipient; she looked only at her own hands, which felt suddenly clumsy and useless. A profound hollowness echoed in the cavern of her chest, a sensation akin to having someone reach into her and gently pull the air out of her lungs. She wanted to drop the vase, to shatter the glass, but a strange paralysis held her rooted to the spot, forcing her to simply stand there, absorbing the sheer, unattached beauty of the offering.

She stared at the bouquet resting on her kitchen counter—a vibrant, almost offensively cheerful arrangement of sunflowers and deep crimson roses. There was no note, just the lush, undeniable presence of the blooms. Her fingers, trembling slightly, traced the velvety texture of a petal, a strange, sharp ache blooming behind her ribs. She hadn't seen Liam in three weeks, and the sight of the flowers felt like a deliberate, cruel punctuation mark on the end of everything. She picked up a stray leaf, crushing it under her thumb, the small act of destruction a futile attempt to disrupt the overwhelming sense of emptiness.

Mark watched the delivery driver leave the package, his gaze fixed on the porch where the vibrant arrangement sat beside the welcome mat. His chest felt tight, a constricted space where his usual steady rhythm had gone erratic. He didn't move toward it. Instead, he leaned against the doorframe, his knuckles white where he gripped the wood. The colors—so bright, so aggressively alive—only amplified the dull, pervasive ache in his core. He could feel a sudden, hot sting behind his eyes, and he blinked rapidly, trying to scrub the sudden moisture away, but the feeling persisted, heavy and suffocating.

She didn't look at the recipient; she looked only at her own hands, which felt suddenly clumsy and useless.
